coredata.dc:title: Corrosion resistant solid-state carbon dots@silicalite-1 composite for latent fingerprints detection
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Zhe
Dai, Hanqing
Zhou, Wenjie
Wei, Jinxin
Zhang, Hongbin
Ye, Zhaoqi
Qiu, Yi
Chen, Yuanyuan
Duan, Zhongtao
Wang, Jing
Zhang, Wanlu
Xie, Fengxian
Guo, Ruiqian
coredata.dc:description: The aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ) properties of the solid-state carbon dots (CDs) limit their wide-scale actual applications. Herein, a novel corrosion resistant solid-state CDs@Silicalite-1 (CDs@S-1) composite was synthesized via in situ embedding CDs into the S-1 matrix. Owing to the confinement effect, CDs are immobilized into the interrupted defects of the three-dimensional S-1 matrix. The as-prepared composite possesses high photoluminescence quantum yield (63%) and extra-high stability in common organic solvents and various pH solutions (pH=2–14). Moreover, the feasibility of the CDs@S-1 composite as a fluorescent label on various substrates was investigated. The results demonstrate the images with well-defined papillary ridges under ultraviolet (UV) light and show great potential for latent fingerprints detection in forensic science.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Aggregation-induced quenching
Solid-state fluorescence
Latent fingerprints detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs@S-1 composite and S-1
Preparation of CDs/S-1 composite by physical mixing
Latent Fingerprints based on CDs@S-1 composite

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 62074044 , NSFC, No. 61904036 and NSFC, No. 61675049 ), Zhongshan - Fudan Joint Innovation Center and Jihua Laboratory Projects of Guangdong Province ( X190111UZ190 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of CDs@S-1 composite powder and its application in LFPs detection.
The images of the CDs@S-1 composite with 3.6 wt% CDs under (a) sunlight and (b) UV light. (c) The absorption and PL emission spectra of the CDs@S-1 composite with 3.6 wt% CDs.

SEM images of the pure S-1 (a) and CDs@S-1 composite with 3.6 wt% CDs (b). (c) HRTEM image of the CDs@S-1 composite with 3.6 wt% CDs. (d) XRD patterns of the CDs@S-1 composite with 3.6 wt% CDs and pure S-1.

(a) FT-IR spectra of the CDs@S-1 composite with 3.6 wt% CDs and pure S-1. (b) N2 sorption isotherms and pore size distribution of the pure S-1, CDs@S-1 composite with 3.6 wt% CDs and CDs/S-1 composite. (c) PL QY of the CDs@S-1 composite versus the loadings of the CDs under 380 nm excitation. (d) The PL spectra, PL QY and the peak position of the CDs solution, CDs@S-1 composite with 3.6 wt% CDs and CDs/S-1 composite taken under 380 nm excitation.
Fluorescence images of the CDs@S-1 composite with 3.6 wt% CDs in various organic solvents (a) and different pH solutions (b).
Latent fingermarks processed with the CDs@S-1 composite with 3.6 wt% CDs powder on various substrates under the UV light. (a) Plastic, (b) Aluminum alloy, (c) Glass and (d)Tinfoil.

head.ce:title: Corrosion resistant solid-state carbon dots@silicalite-1 composite for latent fingerprints detection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Electric Light Sources, School of Information Science and Technology, Fudan University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ) properties of the solid-state carbon dots (CDs) limit their wide-scale actual applications. Herein, a novel corrosion resistant solid-state CDs@Silicalite-1 (CDs@S-1) composite was synthesized via in situ embedding CDs into the S-1 matrix. Owing to the confinement effect, CDs are immobilized into the interrupted defects of the three-dimensional S-1 matrix. The as-prepared composite possesses high photoluminescence quantum yield (63%) and extra-high stability in common organic solvents and various pH solutions (pH=2–14). Moreover, the feasibility of the CDs@S-1 composite as a fluorescent label on various substrates was investigated. The results demonstrate the images with well-defined papillary ridges under ultraviolet (UV) light and show great potential for latent fingerprints detection in forensic science.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, luminescent carbon dots (CDs) have received increasing attention due to their attractive properties, such as low toxicity, low cost, excellent biocompatibility, and so forth [1–4]. These characteristics endow CDs with extensive potential applications in latent fingerprints (LFPs) detection [5], light-emitting diodes [6] and data encryption [7]. However, the aggregation-induced quenching (AIQ) properties of the solid-state CDs limit their wide-scale commercialization. For LFPs detection, the CDs aqueous solutions are limited by the poor stability (only stay stable for 1 day) and are difficult to be utilized on some permeable substrates [8]. Therefore, the solid-state CDs-based composites are suitable for developing LFPs detection. Unfortunately, only a few composites have been reported because they generally present in a fixed shape rather than powder.

Similar to other fluorophores, the main origin of AIQ is determined by non-radiative recombination caused by energy or charge transfer among the aggregated luminescent substances [9]. Hence, many efforts have been made to overcome this problem. Up to now, the reported host matrices can be divided into two categories: one is the dispersed matrix that can conquer aggregation including starch [10], inorganic salts [11], polymers [12], and the other is the nano-porous materials that can confine CDs in three-dimensional frameworks including zeolites [13], metal-organic frameworks [14] and mesoporous materials [15].

Among these, zeolites, as a crucial porous material with a high specific surface area, can not only disperse CDs on the surface but also confine CDs in the nano space. However, it is somewhat difficult to achieve because of the smaller pore size of zeolites (less than 2 nm) compared to the size of CDs. Bourlinos et al. firstly immobilized CDs onto the external surface of NaY zeolite by thermal oxidation [16]. Then, Xiu’s group prepared carbon nanoparticles confined in MAPO-44 zeolite by thermal decomposition of the occluded template or loaded organic molecules [17]. Afterward, Yu’s group developed a “CDs-in-zeolite” strategy to generate a series of CDs-based zeolite composites [13,18,19]. It is worth noting that the selection of a suitable zeolite matrix is the key to generate the CDs-based composite. Silicalite-1 (S-1), as one of low toxicity, low-cost, excellent thermal stability siliceous zeolite with pentagonal 10-membered ring structure as a template, is widely used as a supporting host to encapsulate nanoparticles including Pd [20], Pt [21] and TiO2 [22]. However, to the best of our knowledge, no work has been done to report CDs@silicalite-1 (CDs@S-1) composite by in-situ synthesis.

Herein, we propose a facile synthetic method to obtain CDs@S-1 composite by in situ embedding CDs into the S-1 matrix for the first time. The AIQ of the solid-state CDs has been effectively conquered through the confinement effect of the S-1 matrix. The as-prepared CDs@S-1 composite exhibits high photoluminescence quantum yield (PL QY) of 63% and extra-high stability in common organic solvents and various pH solutions (pH=2–14). Additionally, the composite used as an ideal solid-state fluorescent label for LFPs detection has also been studied ( Fig. 1). These results demonstrate the application potentials in forensic science.

Tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH, 25 wt%), citric acid (CA), Tetrahydrofuran (THF), n-hexane, ethanol, ethyl acetate and hydrochloric acid were of analytical grade and obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Diethylenetriamine (DETA, 99%), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 99%) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, analytical grade) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent (China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), toluene and acetone were of analytical grade and purchased from Shanghai Dahe Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. All chemicals were used directly without further purification.

1 g of CA and 3 mL of DETA were added to 10 mL of deionized water with stirring for 15 min. Then, the formed transparent solution was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon autoclave and heated at 180 ℃ for 6 h. After air-cooling to room temperature, the obtained mixture was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min and dialyzed (MWCO: 500 Da) for 24 h. Finally, the brownish solution was obtained and freeze-dried for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs@S-1 composite and S-1
ce:section.ce:para: The precursor solution was composed of TPAOH, NaOH solution (1 wt%), TEOS and H2O with a ratio of 13.72 g: 2.08 g: 20 g: 40 g. After stirring for 15 min, the solution was mixed with different amounts of CDs (0.15 g, 0.3 g, 0.45 g, 0.6 g, 0.75 g). Afterward, the mixture was loaded in a 250 mL flask and stirred at room temperature for 24 h, then stirred with reflux at 100 ℃ for 24 h. The as-synthesized product was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min and washed with water three times, followed by drying at 60 °C overnight. Finally, the composite was ground into powder for further use.

As for S-1, the CDs were not added to the precursor solution while the rest of the procedures were the same. Especially, to ensure that the only variable is the addition of CDs, the template was not removed by calcination except for BET test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs/S-1 composite by physical mixing
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized CDs (0.45 g) and S-1 (3 g) were mixed with deionized water (70 mL). The mixture was stirred with reflux at 100 ℃ for 24 h. After that, the product was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min and washed with water three times, followed by drying at 60 °C overnight. The solids were ground into powder for further use. Likewise, the template was not removed by calcination except for BET test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Latent Fingerprints based on CDs@S-1 composite
ce:section.ce:para: All the fingerprint samples were from the right thumb of the same male donor. Various substrates including plastic, glass, tinfoil and aluminum alloys were used to collect fingerprints. The powder dusting method was carried out to develop the LFPs by using a soft brush and the LFPs were imaged under ultraviolet (UV) light (365 nm).

Morphological and internal structures were characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM; Tecnai G2 F30, FEI, USA), field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Mira 3 XH, Tescan, Czech) and X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were recorded on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS5 spectrometer using KBr pellets. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were recorded by a K-Alpha electron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The binding energy calibration was based on C 1s at 284.6 eV. The N2 sorption isotherms were measured at 77 K using a Micromeritics ASAP-2010 instrument. Before the test, the samples were calcined at 550 °C for 6 h under an air atmosphere (100 mL min−1) at a heating rate of 2 °C min−1. The loading amounts of the CDs incorporated in the CDs@S-1 composite were evaluated according to the thermal gravimetric analyzer (TGA, STA 449-F3, Netsch, Germany) by comparing the CDs@S-1 composite with the pure S-1 synthesized under the same condition. The samples were tested under an air atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. The calculation procedure is described in the supporting information. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (759S, Shanghai Lengguang, China) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (F97XP, Shanghai Lengguang, China), respectively. The absolute PL QYs were measured using a spectrofluorometer equipped with an integrating sphere under 380 nm excitation (FM-PLUS, HORIBA, USA). The powder was supported by a fixed fixture (1 cm × 0.4 cm × 0.3 cm). The PL decay curves were measured on a time-resolved spectrofluorometer using picosecond pulsed diode lasers (375 nm-EPL) under 380 nm excitation (FLS1000, Edinburgh Instruments). The emission spectra were corrected by the detector responsivity function with wavelength. All spectra were detected with the same slits (1 nm) and PMT sensitivity, respectively.

CA is selected as a carbon source and DETA as a nitrogen source for the synthesis of the CDs through the hydrothermal route. As seen from Fig. S1, the diluted CDs aqueous solution is transparent under sunlight, while it presents a strong blue fluorescence under UV light. The emission wavelength is located at around 451 nm when the excitation wavelength varied from 350 to 400 nm. The PL QY of the as-prepared CDs solution reaches 82%, which is comparable to the value reported in a previous work [23]. Because the aggregation of the pure solid-state CDs could induce luminescence quenching, S-1 is chosen as the matrix for the loading and dispersion of the CDs. In this approach, CDs were added to the precursor solution and it was used as a template like TPAOH. As depicted in Fig. 2a and b, the composite presents as white powder under sunlight. The blue light emitted by the CDs@S-1 composite under the irradiation with UV light was clearly visible, whereas pure S-1 powder had no PL emission under UV light (Fig. S2). Fig. 2c shows the absorption and PL emission spectra of the CDs@S-1 composite with 3.6 wt% CDs. Same as the CDs, the composite exhibits excitation-independent emission properties and the peak position is located at around 453 nm as well. Thus, it can be inferred that the CDs are well dispersed in the S-1 matrix. Nevertheless, the PL QY of the composite (63%) is lower than that of the CDs solution. The reduced PL QY is mainly owing to more energy loss which can be further demonstrated by comparing the PL decay curves. From Fig. S3, the average PL lifetime of the CDs@S-1 composite with 3.6 wt% CDs (8.22 ns) is shorter than that of the CDs solution (14.26 ns). Obviously, the aggregation of CDs is unavoidable and it could not be prevented entirely in the synthesis process.

In addition to the optical properties discussed above, the morphologies of the CDs@S-1 composite were characterized using SEM and HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 3a, the pure S-1 shows uniform particle sizes of about 296 nm. After the CDs are encapsulated in the matrix and attached to the surface, the average size of the composite increases to 320 nm, while the morphologies remain unchanged (Fig. 3b). The HRTEM image in Fig. 3c reveals that the CDs are well dispersed in the S-1 without distinct aggregation and the inset shows the clear lattice fringes of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (101) lattice plane of graphite-like carbon [8]. The XRD patterns of the CDs@S-1 composite with 3.6 wt% CDs and pure S-1 are particularly similar (Fig. 3d). Typical diffraction patterns of MFI-type structure were found in both two samples, indicating that the introduction of the CDs does not change the formation of the S-1 matrix. The five distinct peaks at 7.90°, 8.82°, 23.12°, 23.94° and 24.40° are assigned to (101), (020), (503), (151) and (303) reflection, respectively [22]. Besides, there are no characteristic peaks of the CDs appearing in the CDs@S-1 composite because of the weak diffraction intensity of the CDs.

Furthermore, the changes of surface-related functional groups between the CDs@S-1 composite and pure S-1 were detected by FT-IR spectra and XPS. As shown in Fig. 4a, the main absorption bands at around 1221, 1110, 966, 798 and 561 cm−1 are observed in both two samples. The bands at 1221, 1110 and 798 cm−1 are attributed to the internal asymmetric stretching vibration and external symmetric stretching of Si-O-Si bonds of zeolite [22]. The Si-OH stretching vibration is presented at 966 cm−1. The band at around 561 cm−1 is due to the five-membered ring of pentasil zeolite structure [24]. In the CDs@S-1 FT-IR spectra, a new band belonging to the CO stretching vibration of the CDs emerged at around 1572 cm−1. The intensity of wide vibration bands from 2800 to 3600 cm−1, which can be ascribable to O-H and N-H bonds, was improved slightly compared to the pure S-1. The above FT-IR analysis suggests the presence of surface O- and N-containing functional groups derived from the CDs instead of the pure S-1. In the meantime, the results are also confirmed by XPS analysis (Fig. S4). The two samples are both composed of C, N, O and Si elements. The difference is that the contents of C and N of the CDs@S-1 composite are higher than those of the pure S-1 because of the embedding of the CDs. And also, the peaks of Si 2p and O 1s in the CDs@S-1 composite were shifted to a lower binding energy, which provided further evidence for the embedding of the CDs which decreased the binding energy. In addition, according to the high-resolution N 1s XPS spectrum in Fig. S4, the new peak that appeared at 399 eV is attributed to the pyridinic-N and graphitic-N belonging to the CDs [25].

To further demonstrate that the CDs are successfully encapsulated in the S-1 matrix, the surface area and pore structure parameters were studied by measuring the S-1, CDs@S-1 composite with 3.6 wt% CDs and CDs/S-1 composite using N2 sorption isotherms and pore size distribution (Fig. 4b). The textural data of the calcined samples are listed in Table S1. It can be seen that the three isotherms show the same shape and a high sorption in the p/p0<0.1 region, which is characteristic of typical microporous materials. But the adsorption quantity of the CDs@S-1 composite in p/p0<0.1 region is slightly lower than that of the S-1, corresponding to that micropore surface area (Smicro) and micropore volume (Vmicro) decreased with the addition of the CDs. It indicates that the introduction of the CDs results in the decrease of the S-1 crystallinity. On the other hand, the abundant mesopore formed due to the occupation of the guest CDs, accompanied by the total pore volume rising from 0.288 to 0.340 cm3·g−1. For CDs/S-1, there is a decrease in total pore volume and an increase in Vmicro compared with CDs@S-1, because the CDs are only attached to the external surface. Thus, it can be deduced that the CDs are immobilized into the interrupted defects of the three-dimensional S-1 matrix for CDs@S-1 composite.

To optimize the PL intensity of the CDs@S-1 composite, the PL QYs and PL spectra of the CDs@S-1 composite with different loading amounts of the CDs were measured under 380 nm excitation (Fig. 4c, Fig. S5). With the increase of the loading amounts, the PL QY and PL intensity of the composite firstly increase and then decrease, presenting a maximum at 3.6 wt% loading amount. For the loading amounts of the CDs lower than 3.6 wt%, more CDs are immobilized into the interrupted defects of the three-dimensional S-1 matrix with the increase of the loading amounts. The PL QY is dominated by the loading amount of the CDs. Thus, the PL QY and PL intensity of the composite increase simultaneously. Upon further increase of the CDs loading, the peak position is slightly red-shifted and the PL intensity gradually decreases. This happens because during the fabrication of the composite if the concentration of the CDs is too high, the increasing reabsorption and the AIQ of the CDs occur simultaneously in the composites [26,27]. It is difficult to balance the high PL QY and the high loading fraction. Particularly worth mentioning is that the 3.6 wt% loading amount is much higher than those previously reported [19,27]. To exhibit the advantages of the in-situ method, we also synthesized the CDs/S-1 composite via physical mixing processing. Fig. 4d shows that the PL QY of the CDs/S-1 composite is much lower than that of the CDs@S-1 composite with 3.6 wt% CDs under the 380 nm excitation and the peak position is nearly unchanged, which agrees well with the PL spectra results. It is because the CDs can only be adsorbed on the surface via physical mixing. Thus, we have enough reasons to believe that physical mixing is not effective to restrain the aggregation of the CDs.

For criminal investigation at actual crime scenes, the latent fingermarks are often left on various contaminated substrates. Thus, the corrosion resistance of the composite under a complex environment (strong acid/alkali and organic solvents) is important. Under the protection of the S-1 host, the composite possesses strong aqueous/organic solvents dual resistance. As depicted in Fig. 5, after being soaked in organic solvents for 3 days including toluene, acetone, DMSO, THF, n-hexane, ethanol and ethyl acetate, the CDs@S-1 composite cannot be dissolved and emits strong blue fluorescence under UV light. The stability of the CDs@S-1 composite in solutions with different pH values was also investigated. With the pH ranging systematically from 2 to 14, the fluorescence of the composite remains nearly unchanged. In contrast, Fig. S6 shows that the CDs/S-1 composite was partly dissolved in organic solvents/different pH solutions. Since CDs are only attached to the external surface, the fluorescence of the powder and liquid are difficult to distinguish.

Based on the advantages of the CDs@S-1 composite proved above, we explore the application for LFPs detection. Fig. 6 shows the images of fingerprints by using the CDs@S-1 composite with 3.6 wt% CDs as a fluorescent labeling marker on various substrates including plastic, aluminum alloy, glass and tinfoil. As we can see from the images, the CDs@S-1 powder could be attached to the fingerprint residue without missing any details. It is clear that the papillary ridges of the fingermarks (Fig. 6d) with no background interference can be seen under UV light. And it maintained a fairly high contrast. Thus, the CDs@S-1 composite is an extremely possible substitute for traditional fluorescent powders.

In conclusion, a facile in-situ method for the preparation of the solid-state CDs@S-1 composite is reported in this work. The aggregation in the CDs has been effectively conquered through the confinement effect of the S-1 matrix. The as-prepared CDs@S-1 composite exhibits high PL QY (63%) and extra-high stability in common organic solvents and various pH solutions (pH=2–14). In addition, the composite used as an ideal solid-state fluorescent label for LFPs detection has also been studied. These results demonstrate the CDs@S-1 composite is a valuable candidate for traditional fluorescent powders. And it shows great potential for LFPs detection in forensic science.

Zhe Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – Original Draft. Hanqing Dai: Conceptualization, Investigation. Wenjie Zhou: Software, Formal analysis. Jinxin Wei: Writing – Review & Editing. Hongbin Zhang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Zhaoqi Ye: Software, Formal analysis. Yi Qiu: Formal analysis. Yuanyuan Chen: Software. Zhongtao Duan: Software. Jing Wang: Investigation. Wanlu Zhang: Supervision. Fengxian Xie: Supervision, Project administration. Ruiqian Guo: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, No. 62074044, NSFC, No. 61904036 and NSFC, No. 61675049), Zhongshan - Fudan Joint Innovation Center and Jihua Laboratory Projects of Guangdong Province (X190111UZ190).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2021.161660.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon-based quantum dots with solid-state photoluminescent: mechanism, implementation, and application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Xu, G. Wang, Y. Li, H. Dong, S. Yang, P. He, G. Ding, Carbon-Based Quantum Dots with Solid-State Photoluminescent: Mechanism, Implementation, and Application, Small (2020), 16 2004621.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots-in-matrix boosting intriguing luminescence properties and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Li, B. Wang, H. Zhang, J. Yu, Carbon Dots-in-Matrix Boosting Intriguing Luminescence Properties and Applications, Small 15(32) (2019) 1805504.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon dots in solid-state: from nanostructures to functional devices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Ren, L. Stagi, P. Innocenzi, Fluorescent carbon dots in solid-state: From nanostructures to functional devices, Prog. Solid State Chem. (2020) 100295.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Amphipathic carbon dots with solvent-dependent optical properties and sensing application

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fingerprint imaging using N-doped carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Milenkovic, M. Algarra, C. Alcoholado, M. Cifuentes, J.M. Lázaro-Martínez, E. Rodríguez-Castellón, D. Mutavdžić, K. Radotić, T.J. Bandosz, Fingerprint imaging using N-doped carbon dots, Carbon 144 (2019) 791‐797.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Diamond-like carbon structure-doped carbon dots: a new class of self-quenching-resistant solid-state fluorescence materials toward light-emitting diodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Li, Z. Zhang, J. Ding, Y. Xu, G. Chen, J. Liu, L. Zhao, N. Huang, Z. He, Y. Li, L. Ding, Diamond-like carbon structure-doped carbon dots: A new class of self-quenching-resistant solid-state fluorescence materials toward light-emitting diodes, Carbon 149 (2019) 342‐349.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Efficient room temperature phosphorescence carbon dots: information encryption and dual-channel pH sensing

Colloid Interface Sci. 554 (2019) 344‐352.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Towards efficient solid-state photoluminescence based on carbon-nanodots and starch composites
